eli3: Statistical power is the probability that a test correctly rejects a false null hypothesis: Power = P(reject H₀ | H₁ is true) = 1 − β, where β is the Type II error rate. High power means you're unlikely to miss a real effect. A test with power 0.80 will detect a true effect 80% of the time.

Power depends on four quantities: (1) the true effect size δ (larger effect → easier to detect), (2) sample size n (larger n → higher power), (3) significance level α (higher α → higher power, at the cost of more false positives), and (4) population variability σ (lower σ → higher power). The only one researchers control post-design is n.

In practice, power analysis is done before data collection to determine the required sample size. The standard convention is power ≥ 0.80 (Cohen, 1988), meaning β ≤ 0.20. Under-powered studies waste resources and produce unreliable effect estimates even when they 'succeed' (winner's curse).

whenWhy.title: A priori power analysis: planning sample size
whenWhy.body: Before running an experiment, compute the minimum n needed to detect a specified effect at given α and desired power (1−β). Fix: α (typically 0.05), power (typically 0.80 or 0.90), minimum detectable effect (MDE) or expected effect size, and σ from pilot data or literature. Solve for n using the power formula. Underpowered studies are a primary driver of false negatives and the replication crisis.

whenWhy.title: Post-hoc power: misleading after a null result
whenWhy.body: Computing power after a non-significant result using the observed effect size is circular and uninformative — 'observed power' is a direct function of the p-value: if p > α, observed power < 0.5. The correct post-hoc approach is to report a confidence interval for the effect — if the CI includes the MDE, the study was underpowered for that effect.

whenWhy.title: Sensitivity analysis: what effect sizes are detectable?
whenWhy.body: Given a fixed n and α, compute the power curve as a function of effect size δ. This reveals the minimum detectable effect (MDE) at 80% power. Useful for interpreting studies with fixed n (clinical trial constraints, existing databases) and communicating what the study was and wasn't designed to detect.

whenWhy.title: Power in multiple testing settings
whenWhy.body: With m tests and multiple testing corrections (Bonferroni: α/m), individual test power decreases substantially. Family-wise power (probability of detecting at least one true effect) and per-comparison power must be considered separately. In genomic studies (m ~ 10⁶), very large n is required to maintain power at Bonferroni-corrected levels.

deepDiveIntro: For a one-sample z-test with H₀: μ = μ₀ vs H₁: μ = μ₀ + δ (δ > 0), at significance level α (one-sided): Power = P(Z > z_α − δ√n/σ | μ = μ₀+δ) = Φ(δ√n/σ − z_α), where Φ is the standard normal CDF. Increasing n, δ, or α, or decreasing σ, increases power.

The required sample size for desired power 1−β (two-sided, one-sample): n = (z_{α/2} + z_β)²σ²/δ². For α = 0.05, power = 0.80: (1.96 + 0.842)² ≈ 7.85. For Cohen's d = δ/σ = 0.5 (medium effect): n = 7.85/(0.5²) ≈ 32 per group.

deepDive.heading: Power Formula for z-test
deepDive.body: One-sided right test, H₀: μ = μ₀, H₁: μ = μ₁ > μ₀, effect δ = μ₁ − μ₀. Reject H₀ when Z = (x̄ − μ₀)/(σ/√n) > z_α. Power = P(Z > z_α | μ = μ₁) = P((x̄−μ₁)/(σ/√n) > z_α − δ√n/σ) = 1 − Φ(z_α − δ√n/σ) = Φ(δ√n/σ − z_α). For two-sided test at α: Power ≈ Φ(|δ|√n/σ − z_{α/2}) (ignoring the negligible left tail). [1]

deepDive.heading: Sample Size Formula
deepDive.body: Solving Power = 1−β for n: n = (z_{α/2} + z_β)²σ²/δ² (two-sided). In terms of Cohen's d = δ/σ: n = (z_{α/2} + z_β)²/d². For α = 0.05 (z_{0.025} = 1.96), power = 0.80 (z_{0.20} = 0.842): coefficient = (1.96+0.842)² = 7.85. For d = 0.2 (small): n ≈ 197; d = 0.5 (medium): n ≈ 32; d = 0.8 (large): n ≈ 13 per group (two-sample: double these). [1]
deepDive.formula: n = (z_{α/2} + z_β)² · σ² / δ² = (z_{α/2} + z_β)² / d²

deepDive.heading: Power-Precision Tradeoff
deepDive.body: There is a fundamental tradeoff between Type I error (α) and Type II error (β). Lowering α (more stringent) increases β (less power) for fixed n. The only way to simultaneously reduce both is to increase n. In exploratory research, α = 0.10 or 0.20 may be acceptable to increase power; in confirmatory trials with strong consequences (FDA approval), α = 0.01 with power = 0.90 may be required. [1]

deepDive.heading: Winner's Curse in Underpowered Studies
deepDive.body: When an underpowered study 'succeeds' (rejects H₀), the observed effect size is inflated above the true effect — this is the winner's curse (Ioannidis, 2008). Only extreme sample effects pass the significance threshold in underpowered designs. Meta-analyses that include underpowered studies are subject to this bias. Publication bias (only significant results published) compounds the problem.

takeaways: Power = P(reject H₀ | H₁ true) = 1 − β; convention is power ≥ 0.80. [1]
Power increases with: larger δ (effect size), larger n, larger α, smaller σ. [1]
Sample size: n = (z_{α/2} + z_β)²σ²/δ² for a two-sided z-test. [1]
Post-hoc 'observed power' using the observed effect is circular — report CIs instead. [1]
Winner's curse: significant results from underpowered studies overestimate the true effect size. [1]

edgeCases.q: If power = 0.80, what is the expected false discovery rate among significant results?
edgeCases.a: It depends on the prior probability that H₁ is true (base rate R). Using the positive predictive value formula: PPV = Power·R / (Power·R + α·(1−R)). If R = 0.10 (10% of hypotheses tested are true), α = 0.05, power = 0.80: PPV = 0.80·0.10/(0.80·0.10 + 0.05·0.90) = 0.08/(0.08+0.045) ≈ 0.64. So 36% of significant results are false positives — much higher than the nominal 5%. (Ioannidis, 2005)

edgeCases.q: How does power analysis differ for non-parametric tests?
edgeCases.a: Non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney, Wilcoxon) don't have closed-form power formulas because power depends on the full distribution, not just μ and σ. Simulation-based power analysis is the standard approach: simulate data under H₁, run the test many times, and estimate power as the proportion of rejections. For data close to normal, non-parametric tests have approximately 95% of the power of their parametric counterparts (asymptotic relative efficiency). [1]

sources.label: Cohen, J. — Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed., 1988)
Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 8
